text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the omentum adhered to the anterior abdominal wall during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The gallbladder contains a two-centimeter stone.

The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus using a harmonic device, ensuring the silicon jaw is directed away from the cystic duct to prevent heat transfer. The anterior peritoneum is then opened at the same level by gently knocking with the tip of the harmonic.

The surgeon performs skeletonization to identify the Calot's triangle, focusing on the cystic duct and cystic artery. The peritoneum over these structures is being carefully dissected, with the inferior surface of the liver visible as part of the triangle.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create anterior and posterior windows between the cystic duct and cystic artery. The indentation by the tip and full jaw opening of the Maryland instrument forms approximately a 2.2 cm window, allowing for clip placement and further dissection of the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon hooks the cystic artery from below to create a proper posterior window, defining Calot's triangle. Titanium clips are applied over the cystic duct and cystic artery, with the tips of the clip applicator visible.

The surgeon applies a single clip to the cystic duct and artery, using harmonic energy to seal the structures. Anterolateral and anteromedial traction is applied to separate the gallbladder from the liver, with the neck of the gallbladder positioned towards the falciform ligament. The gallbladder is gradually pulled anteriorly, completing its separation from the liver.

The surgeon applies anteromedial and anterolateral traction to open the peritoneum, revealing a clear areolar plane. The body of the gallbladder is then progressively separated from the liver with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon examines the clipping of the cystic duct and cystic artery before proceeding to separate the final attachment of the gallbladder from the liver using gentle traction and lifting the liver. The gallbladder, containing a stone, is completely removed.
The surgeon inserts a claw forceps through the epigastric port to grasp the gallbladder. An ovum forceps is then placed inside the gallbladder to crush the stone, facilitating its removal.
